Large blur radius used in an animation. Animating blur() values above ~30px forces the GPU to re-composite on every frame, causing significant jank especially on mobile. Prefer smaller blur values (under 20px), or animate opacity/transform instead.

Animation or transition targets a layout-triggering property. Animating layout properties (such as width, height, top, left, margin, padding, or 'all') causes the browser to re-layout the page on every frame, which is computationally expensive and causes jank. Use transform (e.g., scale, translate) and opacity instead.

will-change applied permanently in a static style prop. will-change signals to the browser to promote the element to its own GPU layer, consuming significant GPU memory. Apply it only just before an animation starts and remove it immediately after — permanent will-change is a net performance negative.

express-session config weakens the session cookie security flags. Session cookies should normally be httpOnly so JavaScript cannot read them, and secure in production so they are not sent over plain HTTP. Keep httpOnly enabled and set secure true behind HTTPS.

An await sits directly inside a loop, so the iterations run strictly one after another: each waits for the previous to finish. When the operations are independent (e.g. fetching N urls), this is needlessly slow and should be parallelized with Promise.all(items.map(...)). If each iteration genuinely depends on the previous, or you are intentionally rate-limiting, the serial form is correct.

Synchronous cryptographic operations (e.g. pbkdf2Sync, scryptSync, bcrypt.hashSync, compareSync) run inside a request handler. CPU-intensive sync crypto blocks the single-threaded Node.js event loop, preventing all other concurrent requests from being processed and creating a simple denial-of-service (DoS) vulnerability. Use asynchronous alternatives like bcrypt.hash or crypto.pbkdf2.

fetch() is called from inside a loop. This often creates an N+1 network pattern or forces requests to run one by one when combined with await. Prefer batching, a backend bulk endpoint, or Promise.all over a mapped collection when the requests are independent.

Move the value to an environment variable or a secret manager, rotate the leaked credential immediately, and purge it from git history.

Implement the function, or remove it if it is dead code. A genuine "subclasses must override" placeholder belongs on an abstract method, not a shipped concrete path.

document.cookie is assigned without an obvious Secure attribute. Client-set cookies are also readable from JavaScript, so auth material should normally be set server-side with HttpOnly, Secure, and SameSite. If this is not auth material, still confirm the cookie is intentionally accessible to scripts.
A javascript: URL executes code when the link is activated. Use a real route, button handler, or safe URL instead.

JSON.parse is called on request-controlled input without a local try/catch. A malformed payload can throw synchronously and turn a single bad request into a 500 response or process-level failure, depending on the framework error path. Validate the input or catch SyntaxError before using it.

JSON.parse(JSON.stringify(...)) is used as a deep clone. This is slow on large objects and silently changes values such as Date, Map, Set, undefined, NaN, Infinity, RegExp, functions, and class instances. Prefer structuredClone where available, or use a clone strategy that preserves the data shape you actually need.

A JWT is read with jwt.decode(), which only base64-decodes the token and does NOT check the signature. Any value derived from a decoded-but-unverified token (user id, role, expiry) is attacker-controlled: a forged token passes. Use jwt.verify(token, secret) before trusting the payload. decode() is only safe for reading the header to pick a key, never for authorization decisions.

Direct fetch() call inside a TanStack Start route loader. Loaders run on the client during navigation; using fetch() directly creates client-side waterfalls and bypasses server-side data fetching. Wrap the call in createServerFn() so it runs on the server with full Node.js access.

Calling refetch() inside useEffect couples your query trigger to React's rendering lifecycle. When the queryKey or 'enabled' option changes, TanStack Query re-fetches automatically — useEffect + refetch() is rarely the right pattern and introduces race conditions and double-fetching.

Rest/spread destructuring from useQuery subscribes to every query state field (isFetching, isStale, dataUpdatedAt, ...), causing a re-render whenever any of them changes. Destructure only what you use: const { data, isLoading, error } = useQuery(...).

useQuery is executing an HTTP write operation. Queries are designed for idempotent reads and may refetch automatically; POST, PUT, PATCH, and DELETE belong in useMutation so retries, pending state, and cache updates follow mutation semantics.

This TanStack Query queryFn has a block body but returns no value. Query functions must resolve data; resolving undefined breaks the cache contract and is rejected by current TanStack Query versions. Return the fetched value or throw an error.

QueryClient is created inside a React component or hook, so a new cache can be created on each render. Create it once at module scope, or lazily store it in useState/useRef before passing it to QueryClientProvider.

useMutation is called without cache invalidation or update. When performing a mutation, you should invalidate relevant query caches using queryClient.invalidateQueries() or update query data using queryClient.setQueryData() in onSuccess or onSettled callbacks. Otherwise, the user interface will remain stale and won't reflect the server-side changes.

useQuery is called without specifying a staleTime. By default, staleTime is 0, which means queries are instantly considered stale. For server-side rendered (SSR) or prefetched data, this causes an immediate client-side refetch upon hydration, resulting in duplicate network requests and potential screen flickering. Set a staleTime (e.g. staleTime: 1000 * 60 * 5) to cache and reuse the hydrated data.

Direct document.startViewTransition() call bypasses React's concurrent rendering. In React 19+ use the built-in <ViewTransition> component or wrap state updates with startTransition() / flushSync() correctly. Direct DOM calls can conflict with React's reconciler and cause flicker or missed transitions.

Avoid using flushSync() to force synchronous updates. flushSync() bypasses React's concurrent rendering optimization and triggers synchronous, blocking layout passes. If integrating with View Transitions, use React 19's native startTransition support instead.

Vite dev server is configured to listen on all interfaces. This can expose local development services to the LAN or container network. Bind to localhost unless remote device testing is intentional, and document the reason when host: true is required.

A VITE_* environment variable is named like a secret. Vite exposes every VITE_* value to client-side code, so tokens, passwords, private keys, and credentials with this prefix are shipped to the browser. Rename real secrets without the VITE_ prefix and read them only on the server.
